title: June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19
28 August 2020, 1:00pm
Because of COVID-19, the measurement of Gross domestic product (GDP) in the June 2020 quarter will very likely be the most challenging in the history of the program, both for Stats NZ’s national accountants, for economists attempting to forecast it, and for policymakers, businesspeople, and journalists trying to interpret it.
The GDP’s sources and methods are well documented elsewhere (see: Quarterly gross domestic product: Sources and methods (second edition) , with a further update in Quarterly gross domestic product: Sources and methods (fifth edition) .)
The purpose of this page is to provide an early indication of the additional analysis and data sources that Stats NZ will use to ensure that the June 2020 quarter GDP results reflect the impact of COVID-19 on the New Zealand economy. It is intended to help users of GDP statistics understand our response to key measurement challenges and aid interpretation and use of the statistics. The page will be updated with further detail by Friday 11 September 2020, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: June 2020 quarter on 17 September 2020.
Gross domestic product: June 2020 quarter will measure economic activity through a range of COVID-19 response levels, from the alert level 4 lockdown (which commenced prior to the beginning of the June 2020 quarter), through the easing of domestic restrictions to alert level 1 (which was in effect by the end of the quarter).
The breakdown of days at each alert level during the June 2020 quarter is as follows:
- Alert level 4: 27 days, or approximately 30 percent (1 April – 26 April 2020)
- Alert level 3: 16 days, or approximately 17 percent (27 April 2020 – 12 May 2020)
- Alert level 2: 26 days, or approximately 29 percent (13 May 2020 – 7 June 2020); and
- Alert level 1: 22 days, or approximately 24 percent (8 June 2020 – 30 June 2020)
The sections below explain how Stats NZ is:
- evaluating our use of output indicators, and how we will identify and resolve material changes in the relationship between intermediate consumption and gross output, which is an important assumption underlying our use of these methods
- accounting for timing and conceptual issues affecting the use of employment indicators
- explaining how we are adapting methods that are adversely affected by significant economic shocks, such as interpolation and seasonal adjustment.
- explain where the wage subsidy and income support packages will be observed in the national accounts; and
- provide information on the components of GDP that we are monitoring and where we may depart from our usual methods and data sources.
Adapting the measurement approach to reflect COVID-19’s impact Wage subsidy and other government support packages Relationship between economic indicators and GDP results Seasonal adjustment Summary of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components

## Adapting the measurement approach to reflect COVID-19’s impact
Abrupt changes to activity as a result of changing alert levels during the June 2020 quarter mean that some measurement approaches that perform well during times of relative economic stability require additional scrutiny and in some cases may warrant the use of alternative data sources and compilation methods.
The scope, scale, and pace of economic change caused by COVID-19 and the measures to contain it introduce a degree of complexity to compiling GDP estimates this quarter. This is a challenge shared by national statistical offices around the world. In response we have undertaken a systematic review of our data and methods to identify areas where a different approach and/or the use of alternative data sources are required to adequately reflect changes in economic activity.
In practice, this is an extension to the data confrontation, analysis, and quality assurance processes that are a standard part of compiling high-quality GDP estimates, albeit at a larger scale given the nature of the economic disruption caused by COVID-19. For this reason we consider it useful this quarter to signal key aspects of our approach ahead of the release of the statistics.
Stats NZ’s quarterly GDP estimates are New Zealand’s official measure of economic growth, and reflect a robust and relatively timely estimate of economic activity. As more detailed and complete data about COVID-19’s impact becomes available, including through the 2021 and 2022 annual national accounts cycles, we expect revisions to our initial quarterly estimates. Given the measurement challenges it is likely that these revisions will be of a greater magnitude compared to those observed in previous revision cycles.
More detail about methodological challenges was previously published in COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters .

### Output indicators: Relationships between gross output and intermediate consumption
Most sub-components of GDP production use quarterly output indicators as a proxy for the change in value added. The underlying assumption of stable relationships between gross output, intermediate consumption, and value added can change following abrupt shifts in activity, such as those caused by the alert level 4 lockdown. For example, a business heavily reliant on tourism may have fixed costs remaining near to usual levels but experience a sharp drop in sales. If we use the volume of sales as our quarterly indicator we will not capture the impact of the stable costs, which implies a larger decline in value added. If this effect is significant and not corrected for, growth will be overstated in the quarter. This may also lead to revisions in the future when more complete and accurate estimates of value added become available.
For this reason, and to the extent possible, we will be monitoring how gross output, intermediate consumption, and value added changes in the June 2020 quarter, using sales and purchases data from the Business data collection (BDC). The BDC uses administrative and directly collected financial data from businesses to produce measures of sales, purchases, salaries and wages, and operating profit.
Looking at the sales and purchases data will enable us to understand how gross output, intermediate consumption, and value added have changed in the June 2020 quarter and allow us to incorporate this data in our estimates in cases where these changes are significant. This practice will likely be applied on a case-by-case basis, where the data is deemed of sufficient quality and indicates a material impact.

### Employment indicators
Hours-paid data sourced from the Quarterly employment survey (QES) (released in Labour market statistics ) are used as a quarterly indicator for a range of components in GDP(P) and GDP(E). The use of this data source has two main limitations in the June 2020 quarter:
- It is a mid-quarter estimate, and so for June 2020 quarter will represent pay periods ending on or immediately before 20 May 2020. How representative this is of the whole quarter given the different COVID-19 alert levels in place is questionable.
- QES hours-paid data may deviate substantially from hours worked due to the COVID-19 wage subsidy, and so will not be a suitable proxy for activity in the June 2020 quarter.
See Quarterly employment survey – DataInfo+ .
Alternative data sources will be used for confrontation and, if required, to represent June 2020 quarter activity for components that are usually based on QES indicators.
The Household labour force survey (released in Labour market statistics ) collects actual and usual hours worked. Low-level data is being used to understand how activity changed through the reference weeks of the June 2020 quarter.
See Household labour force survey – DataInfo+ .
Employment indicators (EI) may also be used. The EI uses administrative data to produce measures of filled jobs and gross earnings. Low-level filled jobs data is being used to understand changes in employment during the June 2020 quarter and will be utilised in conjunction with actual and usual hours worked from the Household labour force survey for confrontation, particularly for components which utilise hours paid from the QES or which lack quarterly indicators.
The BDC is an important source of data to understand the level of activity in areas where we usually use QES-based indicators.
Electronic card transactions (ECT) data is also being used to understand the level of activity through the June 2020 quarter.
Employment-based indicators are weaker proxies for value added when compared to other data sources. COVID-19-related measurement challenges have brought forward planned work to review the use of QES indicators and explore updating methodologies to use BDC in the measurement of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components. In June 2020 quarter, some components will use BDC data instead of their usual indicators. Any permanent methods changes will be implemented in September 2020 quarter, in line with our standard timing for incorporating new methodologies and revisions to our timeseries.

### Areas where we lack a quarterly indicator
Interpolation of the annual without a quarterly indicator is used for some lower-level components in GDP(P) and GDP(E). This method is generally only used for smaller components. It assumes little variation in the quarterly change from historical trends and is unable to capture sharp shifts in activity.
We have reviewed all components that use this method and will confront and where appropriate use alternative data sources (BDC, HLFS, EI, ECT data as well as other industry-specific data sources) to estimate these components.

## Wage subsidy and other government support packages
The New Zealand government introduced a range of support packages to assist households and businesses that have been adversely affected by the effects of COVID-19. Most of these will not be observed in measures of GDP(P) or GDP(E), as they are transactions affecting compensation of employees, subsidies on production, and government transfers. Such transactions are measured as part of the annual suite of national accounts, which will become available for the year ended March 2020 in November 2020, and the year ended March 2021 in November 2021.
Key government support packages that will not be observed in quarterly GDP(P) or GDP(E):
- The estimated $13 billon wage subsidy scheme is classified under Other subsidies on production. These are measured in the income measure of GDP.
- The $2.8 billion income support package is classified as social benefits in cash; this package is an increase to existing benefits which are already captured in the annual national accounts. These transactions, as well as other elements of the government support packages, will be observed in future releases of National accounts (income and expenditure) and Government finance statistics (general government) .

## Relationship between economic indicators and GDP results
Key economic indicators which are inputs into quarterly Gross domestic product are published by Stats NZ in the weeks leading up to the release. These include Retail trade survey , Wholesale trade survey , Economic survey of manufacturing , Value of building work put in place , and Balance of payments . See Release calendar for key dates.
While these series are inputs into quarterly Gross domestic product, it is important to understand that coverage, conceptual, and methodological differences may produce differences in related series.
Indicator series cover other concepts, such as the volume of sales, while in GDP(P) the focus is on measuring changes in value added (output minus intermediate consumption).
While output movements are used as the quarterly indicator for a number of sub-industries in GDP(P), aggregate industry movements can differ from those presented in indicator series such as Retail trade survey and the Economic survey of manufacturing. This is because relative contributions of sub-industries to the industry’s value added can differ significantly from their relative contributions to output. In usual periods of activity these differences may not produce significantly different estimates when aggregating to total industry level.
However, in COVID-19-affected quarters the magnitude of change in sub-industries may mean that differences in weighting can translate to larger differences in industry-level movements. Therefore, in such quarters it is important to be mindful of this and we recommend focusing on lower-level results.
The scope and underlying data sources used in GDP(P) may also be different from indicator series. For example, Value of building work put in place collects data on activity in the residential and non-residential building sectors. While it represents a significant amount of activity in the construction industry, it does not capture engineering construction activity. Additional data on infrastructure projects is directly collected and included in GDP(P) to inform our estimates of the construction industry.
All methods used in quarterly GDP are summarised in Quarterly gross domestic product: Sources and methods (second edition) , with a further update in Quarterly gross domestic product: Sources and methods (fifth edition) .
Sharp changes in activity, such as those caused by the alert level 4 lockdown, pose challenges for our usual seasonal adjustment processes. This affected some series in the March 2020 quarter and will be a widespread issue in the June 2020 quarter affecting most components of GDP(P) and GDP(E).
Untreated, large movements in data points will cause our seasonal factors to change and result in unwarranted revisions to our previously published seasonally adjusted series.
Stats NZ’s approach is to apply outliering of unusual data points, so that our historical seasonal factors will not be adversely affected and we can produce credible seasonally adjusted results. All series that were outliered in March 2020 quarter will retain this treatment of the March 2020 quarter data point and are highly likely to have the same treatment applied to the June 2020 quarter data point. There will also be a number of additional series requiring outliering of the June 2020 quarter data point. See Datainfo+ Gross domestic product: March 2020 quarter – adjustments and revisions for a list of series that had outliering applied in the March 2020 quarter.

## Summary of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
Below we note the components of GDP production and GDP expenditure that we will be closely monitoring, areas where we may deviate from the usual quarterly indicators, and the data sources we will be using for this. Please note that the information below is valid as at date of release and is subject to change.
- Output indicators are sourced from Economic survey of manufacturing.
- Gross output and intermediate consumption will be monitored and where appropriate the BDC will be used instead of standard approaches.
- Output indicators are sourced from Value of building work put in place and are directly collected for private-sector heavy and civil construction.
- Gross output and intermediate consumption will be monitored and where appropriate the BDC will be used instead of standard approaches.
- QES indicator is used for the public-sector component of heavy and civil engineering construction. Data from quarterly Local authority statistics and BDC will be used for confrontation and if required adjustment.
- Output indicators are sourced from Wholesale trade survey.
- Gross output and intermediate consumption will be monitored across all sub-industries and where appropriate the BDC will be used instead of standard approaches.

#### Retail trade, accommodation, and food services
- Output indicators are sourced from Retail trade survey.
- Gross output and intermediate consumption will be monitored across all sub-industries; where appropriate the BDC will be used instead of standard approaches.

#### Transport, postal, and warehousing services
- A range of directly collected output indicators (based on data such as road-user charges, freight, and passenger travel volume data) are used to measure the sub-components of the transport industry.
- Gross output and intermediate consumption will be monitored across all sub-industries and where appropriate the BDC will be used instead of standard approaches.

#### Rental, hiring, and real estate services
- A low-level component of this industry – Rental and hiring services (except real estate) – has a quarterly methodology of interpolation of the annual with no quarterly indicator. ECT data was used to estimate activity for the March 2020 quarter. For June 2020 quarter, both ECT and BDC data will be confronted and used where appropriate.

#### Professional, scientific, technical, administrative, and support services
- Advertising, market research, and management services, and computer system design and related services use QES indicators. We are likely to use the movement in sales from the BDC in the June 2020 quarter. We anticipate changing the methodology for these components to using BDC data and will implement this from the September 2020 quarter.
- Travel agency services uses a QES indicator. In March 2020 quarter GST sales data was used to represent the change in activity. In June 2020 quarter we will confront actual hours-worked data from the HLFS, data from EI, and BDC data.
- Employment and other administrative services and building, cleaning, pest control, and other support services use QES indicators. QES will be confronted against BDC, HLFS, and EI data and these data sources may be used to reflect changes in activity.

#### Education and training
- Interpolation without an indicator is used for all education sub-industries.
- Preschool education was estimated in March 2020 quarter using an assumption of no activity for the days of the alert level 4 lockdown, and 100 percent activity at alert level 1. For the June 2020 quarter we have been able to access cumulative attendance hours, which will provide a more direct measurement of activity.
- School education will not be changed, with remote learning assumed to be a direct substitute for face-to-face learning.
- Tertiary education was estimated using student visa data in March 2020 quarter and will be again in June 2020 quarter.
- Adult, community, and other education was adjusted using unit-level information to estimate activity during alert level 4. In June 2020 quarter we will be looking at the BDC as a possible source of information.

#### Healthcare and social assistance
- The market components of hospital services and out-patient services use QES. These components used the BDC in March 2020 quarter and this will again be applied in June 2020 quarter.

#### Arts, recreation, and other services
• In March 2020 quarter adjustments, BDC data was used for the following components which normally use QES indicators:
- Heritage and artistic activities - Sport and recreation activities - Gambling activities - Religious services, and - Civil, professional, and other interest groups.
The same approach will be followed in June 2020 quarter. We anticipate changing the methodology for sub-components of this industry where appropriate to using BDC data and will implement this from the September 2020 quarter.

#### Non-profit organisations serving households
- Interpolation without an indicator is the method used for this component
- Adjustments will be made to March and June 2020 quarters in line with relevant adjustments made to GDP production estimates for arts, recreation and other services, and education.

#### Household consumption expenditure
Series with a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator
- Other household services were adjusted in March 2020 quarter to account for days lost due to the alert level 4 lockdown. For June 2020 quarter we will use BDC data if appropriate.
- Other private transport costs used weighted movements from sub-industries in transport in March 2020 quarter
- Road passenger services and other passenger transport costs were both adjusted using ECT data in March 2020 quarter. In June 2020 quarter we will look at both BDC and ECT data.
- ECT data will be investigated for components of housing and household utilities, and miscellaneous services.
Series which use GDP production industry movements
- Recreational services were adjusted to be consistent with changes to heritage and artistic activities and sport and recreation activities in March 2020 quarter. We will ensure these components are consistent in June 2020 quarter.
- Hospital services and out-patient services were adjusted to align with health and social assistance.
- HCE on education was aligned with the education sub-industries in March 2020 quarter, and this will be continued in June 2020 quarter.

#### Travel credit proportions
- HCE is calculated by deducting net tourist spending from domestic expenditure. The proportions used to distribute overseas tourist expenditure in New Zealand amongst HCE categories are updated infrequently. We will review these proportions and make changes where the existing proportions are no longer representative. The rent component of housing and household utilities is one such component that may be adjusted.
- Tourist spending in New Zealand (used in the calculation of HCE) is normally estimated using data collected via the International visitors survey (IVS). IVS data collection has ceased as a result of border closures introduced from March 2020. A new method for measuring international tourist expenditure has been developed to fill this gap. See Estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand during the June 2020 quarter for further information about this new methodology.

#### Gross fixed capital formation
The local government component of other construction uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In March 2020 quarter an adjustment was made to account for the days in alert level 4 lockdown. In June 2020 quarter, data from the quarterly local authority survey and movements in other sub-categories in other construction will be used to determine an appropriate level of activity.
